_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). General Medical: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency
points: Provision of emergency medical care to the agitated, violent, or uncooperative patient
Maximizing and maintaining safety for the patient, EMS personnel, and others

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients of all ages who are exhibiting agitated or violent behavior, are a danger to self or others and in the sole
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Patients exhibiting agitated or violent behavior due to medical conditions including, but not limited to: a. Head injury
b. Metabolic disorders (e.g., hypoglycemia, hypoxia)

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Don PPE
Do not attempt to enter or control a scene where physical violence or weapons are present
Dispatch law enforcement immediately to secure and maintain scene safety
Urgent de-escalation of patient agitation is imperative in the interest of patient safety as well as for EMS personnel and others on scene

Uncontrolled or poorly controlled patient agitation and physical violence can place the patient at risk for sudden cardiopulmonary arrest due to the following etiologies: a. Delirium with agitated behavior: A postmortem diagnosis of exclusion for sudden death thought to result from metabolic acidosis (most likely from lactate) stemming from physical agitation or physical control measures and potentially exacerbated by stimulant drugs (e.g., cocaine) or alcohol withdrawal
Apply a cardiac monitor as soon as possible, particularly when pharmacologic management medications have been administered

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Direct medical direction should be contacted at any time for advice, especially when patient's level of agitation is such that transport may place all parties at risk
Transport by air is not advised
Stretchers with adequate foam padding, particularly around the head, facilitates patient's ability to self-position the head and neck to maintain airway patency
For patients with key-locking devices, applied by another agency, consider the following options: a. Remove device and replace it with a device that does not require a key
Continuous monitoring of: a. Airway patency

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Etiology of agitated or violent behavior if known
' Patient's medications, other medications or substances found on scene
' Patient's medical history or other historic factors reported by patient, family, or bystanders
' Physical evidence or history of trauma
' Adequate oxygenation by pulse oximetry
' Blood glucose measurement
' Measures taken to establish patient rapport
' Dose, route, and number of doses of pharmacologic management medications administered

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Dispatch law enforcement immediately to secure and maintain scene safety
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
